title: Unfunded Mandates Accountability and Transparency Act of 2025
short_title: Unfunded Mandates Accountability and Transparency Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill makes government agencies do more homework before creating new rules that cost a lot of money or affect many people. Agencies must study how much their new rules will cost and ask businesses and other groups what they think about the changes. A special government office will watch over this process to make sure agencies follow the rules. If agencies don't do their homework properly, courts can step in and make them fix their mistakes.
last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 381.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Unfunded Mandates Accountability and Transparency Act of 2025.

sections.title: Section 2 Regulatory Impact Analyses for Certain Rules
sections.text: This section changes the Unfunded Mandates Reform Act of 1995 in several ways. First, it changes the word tribal to Tribal with a capital T everywhere it appears in the law. Second, it adds a new definition for major rule. A major rule is any government regulation that the Administrator of the Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs decides is likely to cause one of three things. It could have an annual effect on the economy of one hundred million dollars or more. This dollar amount will be adjusted every five years to account for inflation using the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers published by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. It could cause a major increase in costs or prices for consumers, individual industries, Federal State local or Tribal government agencies, or geographic regions. Or it could cause significant harmful effects on competition, employment, investment, productivity, innovation, public health and safety, or the ability of United States based companies to compete with foreign companies in domestic and export markets. Third, this section completely rewrites the requirements for regulatory impact analyses. The term cost means the cost of following the rule and any reasonably expected indirect costs, including lost revenues, that result from a major rule. Before creating any proposed or final major rule, the government agency must prepare and publish an initial and final regulatory impact analysis in the Federal Register. The initial analysis must come with the notice of proposed rulemaking and be open to public comment. The final analysis must come with the final major rule. Each analysis must include several things. It must analyze the expected benefits and costs of the major rule, with numbers whenever possible. It must analyze the benefits and costs of a reasonable number of other regulatory options the agency could choose under the law that gives it authority, including options that use incentives and market based ways to encourage desired behavior, provide information so the public can make choices, or use other flexible regulatory options that give the greatest flexibility in achieving the goals of the authorizing law. It must explain how the major rule follows the requirements of section 205. It must assess how much the costs to State local and Tribal governments may be paid with Federal money or otherwise paid by the Federal Government, and whether Federal resources are available to carry out the major rule. It must estimate any unequal budget effects of the major rule on particular regions of the United States, State local or Tribal governments, types of communities including urban or rural communities, or segments of the private sector. It must estimate the effect of the major rule on job creation or job loss, with numbers whenever possible. It must describe how much the agency consulted with elected representatives of each affected State local or Tribal government under section 204. It must summarize the comments and concerns presented to the agency by State local or Tribal governments either spoken or written. It must summarize how the agency evaluated those comments and concerns. When an analysis is required, the agency must include a summary of the analysis information when it issues the rule. The agency can combine this analysis with other required statements or analyses as long as the combined document meets all the requirements.

sections.title: Section 3 Enhanced Stakeholder Consultation
sections.text: This section changes the consultation requirements in the Unfunded Mandates Reform Act. It adds private sector input to the section heading. It requires agencies to consult with impacted parties in the private sector including small businesses, in addition to State local and Tribal governments. It changes the requirement from consulting only about Federal intergovernmental mandates to consulting about all Federal mandates. The section establishes new guidelines for consultation. Consultations must happen as early as possible before issuing a notice of proposed rulemaking, continue through the final rule stage, and be clearly built into the rulemaking process. Agencies must consult with a wide variety of State local and Tribal officials and impacted parties in the private sector including small businesses. They should consider geographic political and other factors that may create different points of view. Agencies should estimate benefits and costs to help with these consultations. The scope of consultation should match the cost and importance of the Federal mandate being considered. Agencies must try to seek out the views of State local and Tribal governments and impacted parties in the private sector including small businesses on costs benefits and risks. They must ask for ideas about alternative ways to comply and potential flexibilities, and input on whether the Federal regulation will work well with and not duplicate similar laws at other levels of government. Consultations must address the combined impact of regulations on the affected groups. Agencies may accept electronic comments from relevant parties but cannot use those comments as the only way to satisfy these guidelines.

sections.title: Section 4 Maximize Net Benefits or Provide Explanation
sections.text: This section replaces section 205 of the Unfunded Mandates Reform Act with new requirements. The term cost has the same meaning as defined in section 202. Before creating any proposed or final major rule that requires a regulatory impact analysis under section 202, an agency must choose the alternative from those identified and considered that maximizes net benefits. The agency can only consider costs and benefits that fall within the scope of the law that authorizes the rulemaking. An agency can adopt a different alternative only if the Administrator of the Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs approves it and the alternative is adopted for one of two reasons. The first reason is to account for costs or benefits that cannot be measured with numbers, including costs or benefits related to constitutional or civil rights. The agency must identify all such costs and benefits and explain why they justify adopting the alternative. The second reason is to achieve additional benefits or cost reductions. The agency must identify all additional benefits and their associated costs, and all cost reductions and their associated benefits. The agency must explain why the additional benefits justify the additional costs or why the additional cost reductions justify any benefits given up.

sections.title: Section 5 New Authorities and Responsibilities for Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs
sections.text: This section replaces section 208 of the Unfunded Mandates Reform Act with new responsibilities for the Administrator of the Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs. The Administrator must provide meaningful guidance and oversight to ensure that major rules requiring regulatory impact analyses are consistent with the principles and requirements of this title as well as other applicable laws, and do not conflict with the policies or actions of other agencies. If the Administrator determines that an agency's major rules do not comply with these requirements, are not consistent with other applicable laws, or conflict with other agency policies or actions, the Administrator must identify areas of noncompliance, notify the agency, and request that the agency comply before finalizing the major rule. The Administrator must submit to Congress an annual written report. The report goes to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs of the Senate and the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform of the House of Representatives. For the one year period before the report, it must detail how well each agency complied with the requirements of this title that relate to major rules requiring regulatory impact analyses, including activities undertaken at the Administrator's request to improve compliance. The report must contain an appendix detailing how well each agency complied with section 204.

sections.title: Section 6 Initiation of Rulemaking
sections.text: This section renumbers the existing section 209 to section 210 and adds a new section 209 to the Unfunded Mandates Reform Act. When an agency determines to start a rulemaking that may result in a major rule, the agency must do two things. First, it must establish an electronic docket for that rulemaking, which may have a physical counterpart. Second, it must publish a notice of initiation of rulemaking in the Federal Register. This notice must briefly describe the subject and objectives of the major rule and the problem to be solved. It must refer to the legal authority under which the major rule would be proposed, including the specific law that authorizes the rulemaking. It must invite interested people to propose alternatives and other ideas about how to best accomplish the agency's objectives in the most effective way. It must indicate how interested people may submit written material for the docket. The notice must appear in the Federal Register no later than ninety days before the agency publishes a notice of proposed rulemaking for the major rule.

sections.title: Section 7 Inclusion of Application to Independent Regulatory Agencies
sections.text: This section makes two changes. First, it changes the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 to remove the exemption for independent regulatory agencies. Second, it adds a new section 6 to the Unfunded Mandates Reform Act that exempts monetary policy. Nothing in title II, III, or IV of the Unfunded Mandates Reform Act applies to rules about monetary policy proposed or implemented by the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System or the Federal Open Market Committee.

sections.title: Section 8 Judicial Review
sections.text: This section replaces section 401 of the Unfunded Mandates Reform Act with new judicial review provisions. A person who is harmed by final agency action in adopting a major rule that is subject to section 202 can ask a court to review whether the agency followed section 202 about regulatory impact analyses or section 205 about maximizing net benefits. Chapter 7 of title 5 of the United States Code governs how courts review these cases. Each court that has the power to review a rule for compliance with section 553 of title 5 or under any other law also has the power to review a claim brought under this section. When granting relief in an action under this section, a court must order the agency that created the major rule under review to take corrective action consistent with chapter 7 of title 5.

sections.title: Section 9 Applying Substantive Point of Order to Private Sector Mandates
sections.text: This section changes the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 to expand the scope of certain procedural rules in Congress. It changes Federal intergovernmental mandates to Federal mandates and updates a cross reference to include both subsection (a)(1) and (b)(1) of section 424.

sections.title: Section 10 Effective Date
sections.text: Sections 3, 4, 5, and 7 of this Act and the changes made by those sections will take effect one hundred twenty days after this Act becomes law.
